analysis.note: Elliptic adjective/substantive: the commentary supplies parikalpitaḥ svabhāvaḥ.
Instrumental construed with niḥsvabhāvaḥ: ‘natureless in/by respect of defining-character’; fused with eva in external sandhi.
Emphasizes lakṣaṇena; fused with the preceding word in lakṣaṇenaiva.

verification.detail: compound stem niḥsvabhāva as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: The commentary identifies this as paratantrasvabhāvaḥ, the dependent nature.
Pāda-final/pausal spelling is punaḥ; lemma is punar.
Commentarially taken as self-arising/independent coming-to-be, since the explanation immediately invokes parapratyayenotpattiḥ and utpattiniḥsvabhāvatā.

verification.detail: compound stem svayaṃbhāva as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Genitive referring to aparaḥ, i.e. the paratantrasvabhāvaḥ.
Marks the preceding clause, na svayaṃbhāva etasya, as the explanatory statement; in sandhi with etasya and aparā.
Abstract noun in -tā from niḥsvabhāva; here the commentary identifies the specific second type as utpattiniḥsvabhāvatā.

justifications.decision: The masculine prathamaḥ is not taken as modifying the feminine niḥsvabhāvatā; it elliptically denotes the first svabhāva, supplied by Sthiramati as parikalpitaḥ svabhāvaḥ.
options_considered: ‘the first naturelessness’ as the grammatical subject
the first of the three natures, namely parikalpita-svabhāva
justifications.chosen: prathamaḥ refers to the imagined nature (parikalpita-svabhāva).

evidence.quote: prathamaḥ parikalpitaḥ svabhāvo 'yaṃ ca lakṣaṇenaiva niḥsvabhāvas
evidence.translation: ‘First’ means the imagined nature; and this is natureless by the defining-character itself.

justifications.decision: The instrumental is not a positive means (‘by a mark it is natureless’) but the respect in which the parikalpita is natureless: its very lakṣaṇa/svarūpa is projected and absent, like a sky-flower.
options_considered: lakṣaṇa as an external sign or symptom
lakṣaṇa as defining-character/own-form, the respect in which naturelessness holds
justifications.chosen: lakṣaṇenaiva means ‘by/in respect of its defining-character alone,’ because that characteristic is imagined and so lacks own-form.

evidence.quote:
tallakṣaṇasyotprekṣitatvāt
rūpalakṣaṇaṃ rūpam
anubhavalakṣaṇā vedanetyādi
ataś ca svarūpābhāvāt khapuṣpavat svarūpeṇaiva niḥsvabhāvaḥ
evidence.translation: Because its defining-character is imagined. Form is what has form as its characteristic; feeling has experience as its characteristic, and so on. Therefore, because its own-form is absent, like a sky-flower it is natureless by its own-form itself.

justifications.decision: The ‘other’ is not the perfected nature and not an unspecified second item; Sthiramati explicitly glosses aparaḥ punar as paratantrasvabhāvaḥ.
justifications.chosen: aparaḥ punar denotes the dependent nature (paratantra-svabhāva).

evidence.translation: ‘The other, again’ means the dependent nature.

justifications.decision: Although svayaṃbhāva could be translated mechanically as ‘own-being,’ the commentary explains it by dependent origination from other conditions and names the resulting type utpatti-niḥsvabhāvatā. Thus the point is lack of independent arising, not sheer non-existence like the parikalpita.
options_considered: ‘it has no own-being’ as a generic denial of svabhāva
justifications.chosen: na svayaṃbhāva etasya is rendered ‘there is no self-arising of this,’ and aparā niḥsvabhāvatā is the arising-naturelessness of the dependent nature.

evidence.quote:
na svayaṃbhāva etasya māyāvat
parapratyayenotpatteḥ
ataś ca yathā prakhyāti tathāsyotpattir nāstīty ato
'syotpattiniḥsvabhāvatety ucyate
evidence.translation: There is no self-arising of this, as with an illusion, because it arises through another condition. Therefore, because its arising does not exist in the way it appears, this is called its arising-naturelessness.

translation:
The first—the imagined nature (parikalpita-svabhāva)—is natureless (niḥsvabhāva)
by its defining-character (lakṣaṇa) alone.
The other—the dependent nature (paratantra-svabhāva)—again has no self-arising;
“there is no self-arising of this”: that is the other naturelessness (niḥsvabhāvatā).
one_shot_delta: A commentary-blind rendering might make prathamaḥ and aparaḥ refer to two abstract niḥsvabhāvatās; Sthiramati instead identifies them as the parikalpita and paratantra svabhāvas.

It might translate lakṣaṇenaiva as ‘by means of a mark’ or ‘with a characteristic’; the commentary makes it ‘in respect of defining-character/own-form,’ because that characteristic is imagined and absent.

It might render svayaṃbhāva as generic ‘own-being’ and flatten the second case into the first; Sthiramati explains it specifically as absence of independent arising, since the paratantra arises through other conditions.
It might miss that aparā niḥsvabhāvatā is, in Sthiramati’s terminology, utpatti-niḥsvabhāvatā, ‘arising-naturelessness.’
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5 gives punar as the analyzed surface; since the verse has pāda-final/pausal punaḥ, I record surface punaḥ and lemma punar.
ByT5 normalizes the lemma as svayambhāva; I retain the bhāṣya spelling svayaṃbhāva for the stem, noting that anusvāra before bh is an orthographic variant.
ByT5 splits niḥsvabhāvatā as niḥsvabhāva_Cp + tā_SNF; I analyze it as one feminine nominal stem niḥsvabhāvatā, Prathama singular.
